query: do negative enhancers exist in genetics

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Inducible Operon -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanisms by which transcription regulation is determined can be positive or negative. If the regulatory protein is an activator, we can say it is a positive regulation; if the protein is a repressor, the regulation is negative. We can distinguish inducible operons with negative and positive regulation and repressible operons with positive and negative regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Briefly, negatively charged DNA is packaged around a positively charged histone protein octamer, which contains 2 copies of histone proteins H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. [2] This nucleoprotein complex is a nucleosome, the basic unit of chromatin. [3] The nucleosomes of a continuous DNA polymer are connected by linker DNA and the complex is stabilized by histone protein H1. The aggregation of chromatin results in the formation of a chromosome. The chromatin of a chromosome exists as either loose, transcriptionally active euchromatin or dense, transcriptionally inactive heterochromatin. [4] Chemical alterations to histone proteins can induce the formation of either the open euchromatin state, which facilitates gene expression by allowing transcription factors and enzymes to interact with the DNA, or the closed heterochromatin state, which suppresses gene expression by preventing initiation of transcription.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The second epigenetic mechanism is post-translational modifications to histone proteins. These modifications include enzyme-catalyzed acetylation, methylation, phosphorylation, and ubiquitylation, each of which alters the DNA-histone interactions in nucleosomes. [16] Histone acetylation often occurs at positively charged lysine residues which weakens the DNA-histone interactions, thus opening the chromatin and facilitating transcription. [17] For example, acetylation of lysine 9 and lysine 27 on histone 3 (H3K9ac and H3K27ac, respectively) correlates with transcription activation. Histone methylation is more complex as it does not change the histone protein charge and can include the addition of 1-3 methyl groups to lysine and 1-2 methyl groups to arginine. [17] For example, methylation of lysine 4 on histone 3 (H3K4me) is associated with transcription activation while trimethylation of lysine 27 on histone 3 (H3K27me3) correlates with transcription repression. [18] Histone phosphorylation involves the addition of a negative phosphate group to the histone tail, but less is known of its function aside from phosphorylation of H2A(X) playing a role in the response to DNA damage and its subsequent repair. [19] Histone ubiquitylation involves the addition of a large ubiquitin molecule to lysine residues. Examples of histone ubiquitylation include H2AK119ub, which is associated with gene silencing, and H2BK123ub, which is involved in transcription. [17] Aside from the relatively straightforward effect of histone acetylation on gene expression, the effects of other histone modifications are complex and greatly influenced by the state of nearby DNA molecules.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Transposons -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Transposons are a mechanism to increase genomic diversity. Transposons can inactivate or alter gene expression by inserting themselves within introns, exons, and other regions. They can move non-transposon DNA. They can remove genes by internal deletions. [6] There are many mechanisms of transposition that minimize the deletions to promote the survival of the host. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The acetylation and methylation processes are carried out by specific proteins which exist in all of our cells. Histone acetyltransferases (HAT) transfer acetyl groups from acetyl coenzyme A to the lysine molecules. This transfer eliminates the natural positive charge of the histone proteins and therefore reduces its interaction with the negatively charged DNA phosphates, ultimately leading to a euchromatin state and turning on gene expression. Histone deacetylases (HDACs) function to remove acetyl groups from the lysine, leading to a heterochromatin state and turning off gene expression. Histone methylation is not as straightforward as acetylation. The effect of histone methylation depends on the location of the modified position, and the number of methyl groups added. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of patients with Down syndrome have an extra copy of chromosome 21. There are different hypotheses related to the genetic basis of Down syndrome and the association of different genotypes with the phenotypes. Among them is gene dosage imbalance, in which there is an increased dosage or number of genes of Hsa21, which results in increased gene expansion. [4] . It further includes the possibility of association of different genes with different phenotypes of Down syndrome. The other popular hypothesis is the amplified development instability hypothesis, according to which the genetic imbalance created by a number of trisomic genes results in a greater impact on the expression and regulation of many genes. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Current FDA Approved Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Hypomethylating Agents

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: All DNA is packaged as structures called nucleosomes which are the basic unit of chromatin. [3] Nucleosomes are composed of double-stranded DNA wrapped around an octamer of histone proteins, including two of each of the following: H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. The octamer structure is a four-helix bundle comprised of two H3-H4 and two H2A-H2B dimers. [12] An additional histone protein, H1, is not part of the core histone octamer but plays a role in nucleosome stability by acting as a linker protein to further stabilize the nucleosome. [13] Histone proteins have high lysine and arginine content which makes them positively charged. [14] DNA is largely negatively charged, thus favoring interactions with positively charged histone proteins that stabilize the nucleosome. [15] Histone stabilization occurs via numerous protein-protein interactions, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic forces. [2] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anti-Inflammatory Diets -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer is a group of diseases characterized by the dysregulation of cellular proliferation, a process normally regulated by proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes. [12] Modern cancer therapies sometimes target genomic manipulation to prevent or reverse neoplastic growth. Epigenetics refers to reversible, heritable changes in gene expression in the absence of mutation. Examples of stimuli that can cause epigenetic changes include physical activity, tobacco, alcohol, and diet. Epigenetic modifications occur through DNA and histone methylation, histone acetylation, and noncoding RNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There has been increasing interest in epigenetic research due to the possible effects it can have on modifying gene expression in vivo.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Epigenetics is the study of heritable and stable changes in gene expression that occur through alterations in the chromosome rather than in the DNA sequence. [1] Despite not directly altering the DNA sequence, epigenetic mechanisms can regulate gene expression through chemical modifications of DNA bases and changes to the chromosomal superstructure in which DNA is packaged.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Three different epigenetic mechanisms have been identified: DNA methylation, histone modification, and non-coding RNA (ncRNA)-associated gene silencing. Catalyzed by DNA methyltransferase enzymes, DNA methylation involves the addition of a methyl group directly to a cytosine nucleotide within a cytosine-guanine sequence (CpG), which are often surrounded by other CpG’s forming a CpG island. CpG islands are common targets for epigenetic DNA methylation, notably the CpG islands within promoter regions. Indeed, it has been reported that around 70% of gene promotor regions lie within CpG islands. [13] Methylated cytosines within a promoter region recruit gene suppressor proteins and reduce interaction between the DNA and transcription factors. [14] Cytosine methylation also drives the formation of heterochromatin, so the nucleosome tightening prevents transcriptional machinery from interacting with the DNA. [15] As such, DNA methylation within promoter regions results in gene silencing. Cancers often show marked hypermethylation of tumor suppressor genes and hypomethylation of proto-oncogenes, both of which contribute to tumor carcinogenesis. [15] This epigenetic mechanism also plays an important role in tissue-specific gene regulation, genomic imprinting, and X chromosome inactivation. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gene expression tests assess gene activation status in cells, indicating whether genes are active or inactive, with activated genes producing mRNA molecules that serve as templates for protein synthesis. [18] The mRNA produced helps determine which genes are highly active. Too much activity (overexpression) or too little activity (underexpression) of specific genes may suggest particular genetic disorders, including various cancer types. [19] Biochemical tests assess protein or enzyme levels and activity rather than directly analyzing DNA. [20] Abnormalities in these substances may indicate DNA changes underlying a genetic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Editing Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc finger proteins are fused to a DNA cleavage domain to make zinc finger nucleases. These nucleases cause double-stranded breaks at a very specific targeted DNA sequence, which can then disrupt that specific sequence due to errors during the non-homologous repair end-joining pathway, thereby permanently disrupting the targeted gene. The zinc finger nucleases are highly individualized for their respective DNA sequences, and a new one must be engineered for each specific site, making this process too laborious and expensive. [3] Transcription Activator-Like Effectors (TALE)

retrieved_docs.title: Quinolones -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Growing bacterial resistance against quinolones is a rising concern in antibiotic use. There have been three main mechanisms of resistance documented, include target-mediated, plasmid-mediated, and chromosome-mediated resistance. The most common is target-mediated resistance, in which specific mutations in the quinolone target enzymes weaken interactions between the quinolone active site and the enzyme. Plasmid-mediated resistance has become increasingly common, allowing for the horizontal spread of extrachromosomal elements encoding for proteins that can either disrupt quinolone-enzyme interactions, alter the metabolism of the drug, or increase the efflux of the drug from the cell. While it usually only confers a lower level of resistance, it can lead to the continued selection of mutants with even higher levels of quinolone resistance. Chromosome-mediated resistance involves either the underexpression of porins, which normally allow quinolones to enter the cell, or the overexpression of efflux pumps that can remove quinolones from the cell, decrease their intracellular concentrations, and decreasing their efficacy against the bacteria. [2] [3] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: A positive result indicates the presence of a pathogenic or likely pathogenic gene deletion or duplication associated with the studied disease phenotype. A negative result suggests the absence of disease-causing deletions or duplications. However, this outcome does not guarantee overall health or the absence of other medical conditions or genetic disorders. Furthermore, a negative result does not exclude a genetic basis for the disease or eliminate risks for future offspring. In cases where affected family members carry the variant in question, a negative result in the proband excludes a diagnosis. Several factors, including limited genetic knowledge and methodological limitations, can produce a negative result. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, produces a physiologic effect described as increased protein synthesis; this enhancement of protein production, particularly of the survival motor neuron (SMN) protein, underlies its therapeutic benefit in disorders such as spinal muscular atrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a site distinct from the active catalytic region, thereby blocking viral RNA synthesis. Key members of this pharmacologic group include delavirdin, rilpivirine, etravirine, and nevirapine, each of which shares the common mechanism of non‑competitive inhibition of reverse transcriptase. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can enhance viral suppression, reduce the risk of resistance development, and improve overall treatment outcomes for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1484
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is an antiviral agent whose mechanism of action is classified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, meaning it interferes with the synthesis of viral DNA and thereby suppresses viral replication. This pharmacologic property places ganciclovir within the broader category of nucleic acid synthesis inhibitors, highlighting its role in targeting viral replication processes and informing its use in the treatment of infections such as cytomegalovirus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1220
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a pharmacologic class that includes delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapin. All four agents share the same mechanism of action, binding to a distinct allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis without requiring intracellular phosphorylation. Because they act at the same enzymatic target, these drugs are grouped together for clinical decision‑making in combination antiretroviral therapy, allowing clinicians to select appropriate members of the class based on potency, resistance profile, tolerability and drug‑interaction considerations. Their classification as members of the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class underscores their collective role in suppressing HIV replication and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin, a therapeutic agent that promotes fibrinolysis by breaking down fibrin clots. By incorporating the enzymatic activities of both deoxyribonucleases and plasmin, this formulation leverages plasmin’s natural ability to cleave fibrin and deoxyribonucleases’ capacity to degrade nucleic acids, enhancing the overall efficacy of fibrinolysin in clinical settings where clot dissolution is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1705
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serve as the parent class for the therapeutic agent nusinersen, indicating that nusinersen is a specific preparation within this broader category. Nusinersen, in turn, produces increased protein synthesis as a physiologic effect, reflecting its mechanism of action. Understanding that nusinersen belongs to the N [Preparations] family and that it induces increased protein synthesis helps clarify its role in clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: antiviral therapy (e.g., telaprevir). Together, these entities illustrate a comprehensive therapeutic landscape that integrates hierarchical classification, mechanistic specificity, drug‑interaction considerations, diagnostic imaging agents, and a spectrum of antiviral modalities—from nucleoside analogues and entry inhibitors to monoclonal antibodies—each with defined indications and roles essential for optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.
